sentences.text: On the 8th of-- on the morning of the 8th of May, the train stopped.
The end of the war.
Same day the war ended.
And I was on the train.
I passed through Prague.
The middle of the-- of the two weeks.
There are two week period.
We left Leitmeritz for a week.
We were through these terrible times that I was talking about-- shooting and killings.
And when we came through Prague, they actually went through the railroad station of Prague, this was the day that Hitler committed suicide and the-- and the Czechs revolted in Prague.
There was actually a rebellion in Prague against the Germans.
And I was trying to run away.
And I went to a bathroom, and I was trying to see if I can stay there until the train moves.
And they picked me up and they threw me back into the train.
And I stayed on the train until the very end with Sergei.
And--
Total two weeks.
One week before, one week after.
We're talking about the 30th of April, and then we talk about the 8th of May.
And I think that other than minor events that happened-- not minor, but I mean the events that happened, and I would be describe it as a-- as a symbolic, or indicative, or representative.
For example, I was not appearing as a Jew, obviously.
But there was a-- there was a Russian engineer who looked like a Jew, but he wasn't.
And I remember that we used to help him because he had lot of sores.
So we used to change his bandages, and I helped doing that.
And so I-- the German SS came by, one of the crazy guys.
And he says, here is a Jew.
I'm going to shoot him.
So Sergei say to me, well, why don't you say something?
Come on, come on, say something, because I spoke German.
So I says, no, he's not a Jew.
He's fine.
So he walked away.

At the end of the 8th of the May, when we were running out of the train, and there were Germans running away, I mean, the, the, the, the SS were running away, and there was no other force there, it was a no man's land.
This was in a-- well, it's a very long word.
[NON-ENGLISH].
It's actually outside of it.
It's very close to the Austrian border in Czechoslovakia.
And I remember that the Ukrainian stood up and said to me, before I-- we jumped out the train, he says to me, I know you're a Jew, but I didn't do anything to you because you saved the Russian.
And I said, mister, that's nice company I kept.
But anyway, so just to give you an idea of what you were dealing with, what kind of environment you were, what dangers there were to you and any others in any circumstances.
I presumed I helped somebody, if you wanted to know whether I ever did.
And then again we jumped out and we went to a farmer who picked us up.
And he took one look at us.
And there were three of us, Stefan, which is a Pole, myself, and Sergei.
And he took all our clothes in the middle of the field, and burned it, and gave us different clothes because we were full of lice.
And then let us sleep in the barn.
And then the next morning Stefan woke up.
And Sergei slept in another barn.
And Stefan says to-- Sergei, of course, knew who I was.
Stefan didn't.
And Stefan turns to me and says, Max, we suffered this war terribly, but, you know, at least we got rid of the Jews.
And I says, Stefan, I'm afraid that there's one you missed.
And that was the end of Stefan.
I never-- we parted.
And I continued with Sergei through the railroads, which were all burned up, and travelled a little bit of a railroad.
You just grab the-- grabbed onto the railroad train, mostly military trains that were moving through the area.
And I was going north.
Yeah, of course.
Well, Russian-- there was no man's land.
There was-- the Russians were racing to Berlin, and they decided not to move in on the southern front.
And the meantime, the Americans were very nice and they stopped at Pilsen.
They didn't go any further.
So we were left with the no man's land.
As a matter of fact, occupied, by of all people, the Vlasov Army, the army of Russians who volunteered to be in German service.
Ukrainian, mostly.
Vlasov was a Ukrainian general.
Well, Vlasov, and the generals ran away, he went to the American side because they figured if they stay on the Russian side, they will be hanged, all of them.
And the Americans, at least, they had a chance.
And they probably did.
And the poor soldiers stayed behind.
So I remember talking to the soldiers, what should we do?
Should we stay, should we run, should we do it?
Anyway, so there were Russian soldiers.
There was no man's land.
And I started moving to Poland with Sergei.
By the time we got to southern Poland, there was a collection center there for Russians.
And I took Sergei there.
And that's when I had this conversation with Sergei where Sergei was saying to me, Max, you realize that nothing good will come of that.
When I go back, they will kill me.
And I-- I kept telling him, you know, you are crazy and all that.
And 25 years later, when I read Solzhenitsyn's I realized that I was crazy and Sergei was right.
What would I have done it if I knew it?
I probably would have tried to take him with me.
I think, I don't know.
Only spoke Russian.
I was young.
But I certainly had enough a commitment to him to have taken him.
But I was convinced that he's going back.
He had a sister or a brother still surviving in the army, hopefully, to find him.
That was the end of Sergei.
I went to Lodz for three days.
When I was in the city of Karlowitz-- which I remember was at the southern part of Poland when I dropped off Sergei, and I took a train.
And I sat in the train.
And I remember that they started asking me in the train-- Poles were sitting there-- what-- what-- what are you coming from, you know what concentration?
You're fine, you know.
Poles in concentration.
Where's your family,.
And you know, I kept saying, you know, my family, everybody's gone.
Well, before long, they figured out that I'm a Jew.
You know, the Poles did not lose their extended families.
And that was it.
And I walked out, and I took some food, and I came back.
And when I was leaving the train, I remembered a lady ran after me and says, look, I wanted to tell you something.
You are back in Poland now after the war.
I want to tell you, things have not changed.
Please do not admit you are a Jew.
It was a bourgeois lady, of course.
So that was the environment that I came in.
So by the time I came to Lodz, I was not too ecstatic about that place.
And I met Sima at the time.
She was there already.
And she was waiting for her husband to eventually came back.
And she gave me some money.
And she told me that as far as she could recollected, my mother was selected to go to the gas chamber, and that she died.
It turned out, of course, last minute, my mother was shifted to another transport and went to Austria.
So she didn't know that.
And she went to another place, which my mother wasn't there.
So Sima gave me some money.
And I told her, I don't want to stay in Lodz.
I have nothing to do here, and I want to go back to Prague.
I fell in love with Prague.
Undamaged.
Paris of the East Europe.
It was gorgeous.
Caught up on all the movies, by the way, later on.
So I went back.
I was the first man-- I was 15th, or 19th, or 20th of May.
I was the first man in the employment office.
I got a job in a factory and eventually I worked as a-- on a lathe.
Did you need papers for this?
Well, the papers that you had-- that's an interesting thing.
You know, you had a paper.
You, you, you stop at the first United Nations or the first-- this particular Russian center somewhere of registration, and you got yourself a piece of paper saying you were born in Lodz.
And I was going to Poland.
Well, by the time I was going back from Poland to Czechoslovakia, I was scared because I was going the wrong way.
I was born in Poland.
Why am I going to go to Czechoslovakia?
So I remember I was sitting in a train with my feet down.
And all the Ukrainians were going back to Ukraine.
And I was sitting there and I see a soldier standing there.
And the looks of him was definitely Jewish.
Russian soldier.
So I said, well, [CLEARING THROAT] and eventually I got him to talk.
So I says, look, I'm going the wrong way.
What will happen to me when they look at me and my papers?
You know, I'm born in Poland.
And he says-- so I remember I blurted out and I said, well, maybe, will they send me to Siberia?
And he says to me, in Siberia, in Siberia you'll make a fortune if you go there.
He had a whole-- he would lift his arm, and he had got watches, one after the other.
He was collecting watches.
So this is the type of people you-- you kind of met at the time.
Well, I made it.
I made it through the pass.
I spoke a little bit Czechish.
I just-- again, you had hordes of people moving in.
And I moved back to Prague.
I got a job.
I loved it.
I was obsessed with normalcy.
I didn't want to have anything to do with it.
I wanted to be normal, normal, normal.
That's all I wanted.
I went to look at the Charles University.
I said, maybe someday I'll go there.
And I went to-- I got the job.
I got myself some coupons for food and clothing.
And I bought myself a suit with a tie.
Every day after work, I would wash up, and I would go out with a suit.
I didn't want anything to do with people in-- in leather jackets, and boots, and refugee kind of a style.
I wanted to be a normal person.
It became an obsession with me.
I wanted to be normal.
And I was.
And I caught up on all the movies, as I said.
And it was wonderful.
And I waited for communication to open.
And the first thing they did is in July they opened the wire services to both the Soviet Union and the United States.
And since I knew that my uncle lived in Oklahoma in a small town, Carnegie.
If you drive through Carnegie very fast, you can miss it.
I mean, it's 2,000 population.
And so obviously, he was there for 60 years or 50 years, whatever it was, since 1905 or 1908.
So obviously it was no problem, just write down Abe Grad, Carnegie, Oklahoma.
So I did.
I wrote a wire to him.
I also remembered that my father had very prominent relatives in Moscow.
Because after the war, I'll have to go back.
Historically, remember, the Russians and the Germans made an agreement.
This is what made World War II possible.
So they actually wrote us letters and sent us packages for the first two years when we were in Poland, in-- in the ghetto.
And even in ghetto, we were getting packages from-- from Soviet Union.
But-- so I wrote to him, and I never got an answer.
Obviously, now I know why.
You don't communicate with-- with relatives outside of the Soviet Union.
So anyway, the next day, or two days later, I got a wire from my uncle saying, mother alive, Rome, and en route, Palestine.
I remember this.
So obviously, I packed up.
And I said, well, that's it.
I'm going.
And I went to the office.
Of course, I was in Czechoslovakia.
I was born in Poland.
The Czechs were tolerating me as a Pole, but they certainly are not going to tolerate me, the Russians, going the other wrong way.
So I walked in and I said-- at some office of registration-- and I said, I am born in [NON-ENGLISH].
The name kind of appealed to me.
And that I just came out of a hospital.
I'm a refugee.
Whatever it was necessary.
I dressed properly, obviously, for that.
I didn't go in a suit and tie.
And they gave me papers.
And I took off.
I met some friends that I lived with in one room.
By the way, I lived in a room, and I had a bed, which I shared with a baker.
He slept at daytime, I slept at night.
We had our own sheets.
So that's the style of living that I had.
Even, it was good days.
But that was OK.
It was wonderful compared to everything else before.
And I went to-- it's OK?
Yeah.
And I went to-- to the border.
At that time Czechoslovakia was still divided.
There was a demarcation line.
Eventually, the Americans moved away and gave the Russians the entire Czechoslovakia.
But at that time there was a demarcation line, and I crossed the demarcation line.
And at that time, I was well dressed, and I sat in A car.
But I decided I'm going to sound German.
So I refused to talk to anybody Czechish.
I spoke only German.
Well, sure enough, the Russian soldiers walked in and said, any Germans here?
Pointed right at me.
And of course, I was taken down.
And of course, I started talking pretty fast that I'm not a German.
And the-- the Russian lieutenant, who was half drunk, somehow or other accepted me just because I spoke Russian.
And says, look, if I see you once more, I'm going to shoot.
So, I don't want to see you anymore.
I said, well read it.
I'm from-- he said, I don't read Czechish.
Bring it in Russian.
So I went back to Czechoslovak, to Prague.
And I went to the office and I said, can I have it in Russian?
He says, no we cannot have it in-- you cannot have it in Russian.
But you can go across the street.
They'll give you a translation for it.
So I walked in and to my surprise, on the door, it was written that it was the previous-- the predecessor of KBG, the NKVD.
And they were sitting there.
By the way the, Russians were not allowed to be in Prague, except the officers.
The-- the Russians have a very great-- had a sensitivty to-- I mean, were very sensitive to Czechish feelings before the war.
And they knew how Russian soldiers behave.
They didn't want to spoil it.
Czechs were the only ones that were very friendly to the Russians, because they remembered what happened during Munich.
They was the-- the only people that protect-- that sided with Czechs were the Russians.
And at that time, of course, it didn't work out.
So the Czechs remembered it.
Of course, not today, but at that time.
So there were very few Russians soldiers in there.
But there were officers and there were offices.
They translated it for me.
But I went to the train again.
This time I sat with the Czechs.
I talked Czechish.
I played with the kids.
The Russian soldier would come in.
Any Germans?
No Germans.
I went through.
Didn't need any papers, anything.
And I traveled primarily through different auspices, under auspices.
An American captain would give me a lift from a jeep from this city to that city.
I wound up in border of Switzerland, Bregenz, in Austria.
From there, I went to Innsbruck.
From there, I went to-- every time I went with a wire.
And of course, the Americans were always very helpful.
The French were terrible.
They wouldn't want.
They want-- first of all, if you don't speak French, you don't-- you show a Frenchman an American-- an English telegram, and you're asking for trouble.
So I didn't have any-- the French were in charge of part of Austria.
And so then I went to-- wound up in Innsbruck.
From there, I went to-- to Bremen Passage, which is the Alps to Italy.
And there, of course, I couldn't get any transportation at all.
I was actually riding in the locomotive with the guy who was moving the locomotive towards the border.
And then I came across a Polish-- Polish Army-- a Dutchman.
And I sat, and it took me to Bologna.
And I sat with Polish officers as a fellow Pole, having a wonderful time.
If they knew I was a Jew, I would be under the train.
And I wound up in Bologna.
And I went to Rome.
From there, it was not difficult to get to Rome.
And I looked for my mother.
And I went-- met an English-- I spoke English, so I met an American lady, who knew a major-- British major who was in charge of refugees.
And he tried to help me.
But you see, the way they worked-- it worked after the war, people were moving, particularly Jews-- any refugees-- moved from the north, from the German Austria, into the south.
And every time they went to a city, they would registered.
But if my mother didn't pass through Rome, she wouldn't be in the Rome register.
So the British didn't have any record.

I mean, the-- the people who would-- and the reason why my mother had a letter from Rome, my uncle concluded, because she gave it to an Ameican soldier, or an American officer, who shipped it from Rome because he happened to be in Rome.
And it had nothing to do with where my mother was.
And my uncle drew the conclusion, she was in Rome.
So anyway, I collected my ration coupons in the Forum Romanum.
I was very-- not very inclined to worry, but I went back to visit it several-- twice.
But at that time, I wasn't too inclined to-- to look at the historical sites of Rome.
And once I walked out from the major's office-- as a matter of fact, an interesting commentary on the opinions of both Gentiles and Jews about themselves and each grouping.
And that is, when I walked into the major the first time, I gave him the letter from the lady.
And he walked out and he said-- I gave it to the adjutant.
Then he walked out and says to me, well, I'm sorry, I need somebody to talk to you.
And I said, I speak English.
So he looks at my papers.
Of course, they were false papers saying that I'm German.
He says, oh, of course, a German Jew.
I should have known that.
So anyway, this tells you a little bit about the-- if you're a Polish Jew, you don't speak English.
But if you're a German Jew, you most likely speak English.
So there is all kinds of classifications that we all like to have.
I think I have-- I have been cured of that.
I treat everybody-- everybody has the inalienable right to be treated by himself, about himself, and of himself.
But anyway, and then I went-- walked out from one's office one day, and I met some people, some men.
And he says to me, what are you doing here?
And I said, looking for my mother.
What's her name?
He says, I saw her yesterday, north there.
And I took the train and I showed up.
And I showed up, and my mother, of course, was ecstatic, to say the least.
She was in a villa that was just taken by the-- by the Jewish Brigade in the British Army, and designated to be a staging ground for sending people illegally to Palestine.
My mother was the oldest.
She was in charge of all the adopted daughters and sons.
Some of them became my best friends, my closest friends.
And one of the girls who I became friendly for a while was, I remember, I was telling Judy about it.
It was Yom Kippur eve.
They prepare Yom Kippur service and meal, particularly meal.
And I remember that it was a beautiful villa.
There was a wonderful dining room with big chandeliers.
And my mother and I were walking down the steps.
I was dressed in a tie and a suit.
So normal.
And I remember I-- talking to my friend.
She's now in Vancouver, quite prosperous.
We talked about it two years ago when we visited her.
And I looked at her eyes.
I remembered her eyes.
She was a redhead.
And the whole horror of the Holocaust was sort of in a nutshell when she saw me with a mother.
And all of them, without an exception, had no mother, no father, nobody.
I think that that stayed with me.
And maybe my quest for normalcy, my pathological quest for normalcy, was helped a lot-- satisfied-- that I found a mother.
Because I wasn't really anymore one of them.
I was normal.
I had a mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was aware of it all the time, the rest of my life.
All right.
We went illegally in December '45.
We were shipped to Palestine.
They caught us.
At that time, there was no Cyprus yet.
There was no big wave or the second ship that was caught.
And we were taken to a prison next to Haifa for two weeks.
It wasn't bad.
It was a camp, a military camp.
We lived there in the barracks.
And they deducted the certificates.
They had a quota, you know, for Jews to enter Palestine since 1939-- $1,500 a month.
So they deducted the-- the quotas, and they gave us legit-- we became legal residents of Palestine.
And we immediately went to a kibbutz, which was a kibbutz where my mother felt very good about because it was formed by primarily Polish and Austrian Jews.
A lot of Polish spoken.
A lot of them from my high school.
So she found herself completely at home, very happy.
Found a home.
And-- and what spoiled the thing is my ambitions.
I was blind with ambition that I wasn't going that war to let me down.
I was going to make something of myself.
Kibbutz was very good to me.
I worked there.
And when I-- when they knew that I was studying, I was studying for matriculation papers, because I didn't have a high school diploma.
So there was a service for the British colonial service.
There was a British matriculation papers, the London matriculation papers.
You could get a high school degree by correspondence.
So I studied.
I remember, I-- after 14 hours in the field or 10 hours in the field, I would study with the oil lamp in the tent and cry, and study.
By the way, it worked out very well.
I want to tell you something about that, too, if we have time.
Something, maybe a lesson about what does-- how you should study, and what does it mean to learn.

But anyway, I-- they wanted me, even after a while, it looked like the kibbutz would even consider, because they knew I was so obsessed with going to college that they decided maybe they'll send me to college.
And there was a rumor about that.
And I could probably pursue it.
But I just felt that I cannot make any commitment.
This is a terrible moral commitment.
I mean, there was no contract that you sign.
But if a kibbutz sends you, in those days-- you know, this is not today where everybody goes to college.
Very few people went to college.
The whole technical college in Haifa had 600 people, of which the entering class was 150.
So you-- you just did not go to college as a routine.
And so for them to send me to college and maintain me and my mother for four years, I felt that I will owe them my life.
And I had no right to take it if I was not convinced that I'll stay the rest of my life, which I was not.
And I would have to study subjects that are useful for the kibbutz, which is mechanical engineering or civil.
So that's about-- I don't know what events I want to relay.
You have something?
Yeah, let's skip ahead to how you got to America.
OK.
So I went to-- we left the kibbutz in '46-- '47, January.
And we-- and I studied, and I got to Technion in '47, in October.
And the partition decision in the United Nations was in November.
And we were drafted, practically immediately.
For a while, we were sort of [NON-ENGLISH] kind of thing for three months.
And they marched, they closed the University.
And I went to war.

I would like to say something about-- you asked me a question before we started, how does an immigrant and how, as an immigrant, as a permanent immigrant in so many places, how did I feel, how was I treated?
Immigrants are never treated well.
They are never-- nobody understands an immigrant.
I'm very sensitive to that when I see others.
But the one thing that I owe Israel, and I will never forget-- never-- and that is, in the quest for normalcy, they gave me a gun, and they let me participate in a war as a soldier in a free country.
And that was the most significant catharsis of my problem of quest for normalcy.
And that is so, while you may think, you know, that I am thanking a society or a group for sending me to war, in effect, they did the best thing they could have done for me, psychologically speaking.
And I'm always going to-- Well, of course, I made friends, obviously.
And became part of the country.
And I mean, participating in-- in an event that happens one in 2,000 years, So this is nothing to-- to-- to gloss over, either.
But I think that from a personal point of view, it was that-- that particular aspect of it that I could come back to normalcy.
Then I can be a GI, and I actually was paid for my education as a GI at Technion.
Because it-- this was-- this was a return to normalcy.
You don't think about GIs in America as normalcy.
Maybe you don't.
Maybe you think that there was a war, too.
But that's normalcy, the one I'm talking about.
Not the Auschwitz normalcy.
So that's about at the end of my-- my BS degree, June-- in the summer of 1952.
My mother didn't want to leave because she felt at home.
She learned a little Hebrew, but she primarily had a lot of friends.
And she felt very secure there, and going to America for her was frightening.
She didn't speak English.
But she had two brothers whom she never saw.
Well, I had-- I had obviously a lollipop in front of her to hold.
She had to see the brothers.
And so she agreed, and we came here.
And we came in October 1952 to Oklahoma.
And I started there going to school and working as a designer in a company named Folding Carrier Corporation.
And I want you all to remember that, because every time you go to a grocery store, that's where we make the grocery baskets.
So the biggest company for grocery baskets.
I think at that time, the only one.
And then I decided that Oklahoma was not really a place where I wanted to be.
It was a little bit of a cultural shock at that time.
Oklahoma today is, by the way, a very fine university, and-- and quite different.
My classmate is-- whom I found recently, which survived, is a professor in Oklahoma University in electrical engineering, which says something.
One of the three survivors.
But anyway, and I met him.
Very, very synergistically.
But anyway, so I didn't think so.
I had to go to the east.
And in order to go to the east, we-- my uncle helped us to pack things.
We took the Greyhound bus.
But on the way, my mother had an adopted-- one of her adopted daughters from Israel was living here.
So we said, OK, we'll stop and see her.
We stopped and see her.
And for a lark, I decided to go and look for a job.
See how it-- looking for a job.
So I got an offer.
And then I continued to New York, obviously.
And that was the end of Korea, Korean War.
And there was a-- it was not a depression, certainly it was a recession.
Couldn't get a job for two weeks in New York, and we had only $200 to our name.
So I called up Chicago.
And I said, I'm coming to get the job, take the job, which I did.
And I always liked it, because I can't stand New York.
So I became a-- a real Chicagoan, yes.
And I love it, because it has both a metropolitan flavor to it, as well as a-- as a sort of a quieter life.
It's a little bit more-- New York gives me a headache.
And how did you meet your wife, and when?
She was-- I worked at-- I went to IIT, which is the school on the south side called the Illinois Institute of Technology.
By the way, I always went to IIT because I also got my undergraduate in Israel Institute of Technology.
So if you see any symbolism to that.
But anyway, so I went to IIT.
And I worked in Admiral for one year.
Admiral used to make television, one of the largest companies in television at the time.
And in '54, I was got offered a job as an instructor and a-- well, I was a graduate student, but as an instructor at IIT.
And I worked there until '58, first as an instructor, and then as a research engineer.

And then I moved to what's called Armour Research Foundation, which is the Illinois Technology Research Institute-- the IIT Research Institute today used to be called Armour Research Foundation in '58.
And I worked there as a research engineer, and I got my doctorate.
In '63, I got the degree, but I left school already with all the requirements in '58.
Judy came to work as a computer programmer in 1962.
And there was a cafeteria, and we met.
September 1963.
And I have three children.
And we had them pretty fast after each other.
Judy was very young, obviously.
But I was not.
My mother was very concerned about it.
And I considered my concern about my children not getting married today, I call it my mother's revenge.
But of course.
